summary: In a co-authored epidemiological study, Julie E. Buring and colleagues demonstrate that while premature paternal history of myocardial infarction (MI) is a strong predictor of cardiovascular disease, any maternal history of MI—even at older ages—confers significant risk. The findings challenge the standard clinical assumption that only early-onset family history is relevant for assessing cardiovascular risk.

principles.statement: Any maternal history of myocardial infarction, regardless of the mother's age at the event, provides clinically relevant information for assessing CVD risk.
principles.rationale: Because women generally experience MIs at older ages than men, restricting the definition of a 'positive family history' to early-onset events (e.g., before age 60) severely underestimates the risk conferred by maternal history.
principles.source_quote: Therefore, any maternal history of MI, regardless of age at the event, may provide clinically relevant information in the assessment of CVD risk.

principles.statement: Premature paternal history of MI is a strong, independent predictor of cardiovascular disease.
principles.rationale: Data shows that younger paternal ages at MI confer greater magnitudes of CVD risk in offspring, and this risk decreases as the paternal age at MI increases.
principles.source_quote: A premature paternal history of MI was an important, independent predictor of CVD in both men and women.

heuristics: Treat any maternal history of MI as a valid cardiovascular risk factor, rather than only looking for premature maternal MI.

signature_quotes.text: Therefore, any maternal history of MI, regardless of age at the event, may provide clinically relevant information in the assessment of CVD risk.
signature_quotes.context: Concluding the clinical implications of the study's findings regarding maternal age at MI.

signature_quotes.text: Therefore, any maternal history of MI may provide valuable clinical information, perhaps challenging the assumption that only an early maternal history of MI is important and warranting other studies.
signature_quotes.context: Challenging standard clinical definitions of family history.

anti_patterns.description: Defining a 'positive family history' of myocardial infarction strictly by early onset (e.g., before age 60).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It neglects the natural age distribution of MI in women, causing clinicians to miss the significant cardiovascular disease risk conferred by a maternal history of MI at older ages.
